sections.heading: RT
sections.content: Radiation therapy is used as a penile-sparing treatment for early-stage disease, as adjuvant therapy for positive surgical margins or nodal disease, and for palliation in metastatic settings. It is an alternative to surgery when organ preservation is desired or for patients who are not surgical candidates. Principles: 1. Circumcision should always precede radiation therapy to prevent radiation-related complications like phimosis or fibrosis. 2. For primary penile lesions, radiation therapy can be delivered as brachytherapy with interstitial implant or external beam radiation therapy (EBRT). 3. Adjuvant radiation is recommended for positive margins, pN2–3 disease, or extranodal extension after lymph node dissection. 4. Chemoradiation is an option for unresectable or bulky disease, using concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy. Dose Frameworks: [Brachytherapy for primary lesion: total dose 70 Gy, continuous low-dose rate over 5–7 days, single implant, indication T1–2, N0 tumors <4 cm for penile preservation (category 2B); EBRT for primary lesion: total dose 65–70 Gy, dose per fraction 1.8–2 Gy, 33–39 fractions, 5 days per week over 6–7 weeks, indication T1–2, N0 tumors or post-surgical positive margins; Adjuvant EBRT for lymph nodes: total dose 45–50.4 Gy, dose per fraction 1.8 Gy, 25–28 fractions, 5 days per week over 5 weeks, indication inguinal and pelvic lymph nodes after dissection; Boost for gross nodes or extranodal extension: total dose 65–70 Gy, boost over 3–4 weeks after initial EBRT, indication bulky nodes or extranodal extension] Approaches: [Primary radiation/chemoradiation for penile preservation: dose fractionation EBRT 65–70 Gy or brachytherapy 70 Gy, concurrent chemotherapy Cisplatin ± fluorouracil (category 3), indication T1–2, N0 tumors <4 cm; tumors ≥4 cm or node-positive unresectable, key trial Crook et al. reported 10-year cause-specific survival of 84% for T1–2 lesions treated with brachytherapy, toxicities Urethral stricture, penile fibrosis, skin necrosis, erectile dysfunction; Adjuvant chemoradiation for node-positive disease: dose fractionation EBRT 45–50.4 Gy to inguinal/pelvic nodes, boost to 65–70 Gy, concurrent chemotherapy Cisplatin, fluorouracil, or capecitabine, indication Palpable bulky inguinal nodes, enlarged pelvic nodes, pN2–3 disease, key trial Limited data; extrapolated from anal and vulvar cancer studies, toxicities Myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity, neuropathy, mucositis; Palliative radiation: dose fractionation 30 Gy in 10 fractions, no concurrent chemotherapy, indication Symptomatic metastatic disease for local control, toxicities Fatigue, skin irritation]

timeline.title: Regimen
timeline.body: EBRT for primary: 65-70 Gy in 33-39 fractions (1.8-2 Gy/fx) over 6-7 weeks; Brachytherapy: 70 Gy continuous low-dose rate over 5-7 days; Adjuvant EBRT: 45-50.4 Gy in 25-28 fractions; Palliative: 30 Gy in 10 fractions
